html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Eleven — Part One

The Book of Shofetim

SHOFETIM

The Book of Judges

Chapters 1 through 7

The Incomplete Conquest — Otni'el — Ehud — Devorah — Gid'on

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

4QJudga (4Q49) — Cave 4 — Primary DSS witness for Shofetim

4QJudgb (4Q50) — Cave 4 — Supporting DSS fragments

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — Shofetim preserved in full

YHWH and The Creator only — no substitute titles

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

SHOFETIM — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 7 — THE EARLY JUDGES

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 1 After Yehoshua Died — Yehudah Goes Up First — The Nations Not Driven Out

1 After the death of Yehoshua, the people of Yisra'EL inquired of YHWH, Who shall go up first for us against the Kena'anites, to fight against them?

2 YHWH said, Yehudah shall go up — behold, I have given the land into his hand.

3 And Yehudah said to Shim'on his brother, Come up with me into the territory allotted to me, that we may fight against the Kena'anites. And I likewise will go with you into the territory allotted to you. So Shim'on went with him.

4 Then Yehudah went up and YHWH gave the Kena'anites and the Perizzites into their hand, and they defeated ten thousand of them at Bezek.

5 They found Adoni-Bezek at Bezek and fought against him and defeated the Kena'anites and the Perizzites.

6 Adoni-Bezek fled, but they pursued him and caught him and cut off his thumbs and his big toes.

7 And Adoni-Bezek said, Seventy kings with their thumbs and their big toes cut off used to pick up scraps under my table. As I have done, so Creator has repaid me. And they brought him to Yerushalayim, and he died there.

8 And the people of Yehudah fought against Yerushalayim and captured it and struck it with the edge of the sword and set the city on fire.

9 And afterward the people of Yehudah went down to fight against the Kena'anites who lived in the hill country, in the Negev, and in the lowland.

10 And Yehudah went against the Kena'anites who lived in Khevron — now the name of Khevron was formerly Kiryat-Arba — and they defeated Sheshai and Akhiman and Talmai.

11 From there they went against the inhabitants of Devir. The name of Devir was formerly Kiryat-Sefer.

12 And Kalev said, Whoever strikes Kiryat-Sefer and captures it, I will give him Akhsah my daughter as wife.

13 And Otni'el the son of Kenaz, Kalev's younger brother, captured it. And he gave him Akhsah his daughter as wife.

14 When she came to him, she urged him to ask her father for a field. And she dismounted from her donkey, and Kalev said to her, What do you want?

15 She said to him, Give me a blessing. Since you have given me the land of the Negev, give me also springs of water. And Kalev gave her the upper springs and the lower springs.

16 And the descendants of the Kenite, Moshe's father-in-law, went up with the people of Yehudah from the city of palms into the wilderness of Yehudah, which lies in the Negev near Arad, and they went and settled with the people.

17 And Yehudah went with Shim'on his brother, and they defeated the Kena'anites who inhabited Tzefat and devoted it to destruction. So the name of the city was called Khormah.

18 Yehudah also captured Azzah with its territory, and Ashkelon with its territory, and Ekron with its territory.

19 And YHWH was with Yehudah, and he took possession of the hill country, but he could not drive out the inhabitants of the plain because they had chariots of iron.

20 And Khevron was given to Kalev, as Moshe had said. And he drove out from it the three sons of Anak.

21 But the people of Binyamin did not drive out the Yevusites who lived in Yerushalayim, so the Yevusites have lived with the people of Binyamin in Yerushalayim to this day.

22 The house of Yosef also went up against Beit-El, and YHWH was with them.

23 And the house of Yosef scouted out Beit-El. Now the name of the city was formerly Luz.

24 And the spies saw a man coming out of the city, and they said to him, Please show us the way into the city, and we will deal kindly with you.

25 And he showed them the way into the city. And they struck the city with the edge of the sword, but they let the man and all his family go.

26 And the man went to the land of the Khittites and built a city and called its name Luz. That is its name to this day.

27 Menasheh did not drive out the inhabitants of Beit-She'an and its villages, or Ta'anakh and its villages, or the inhabitants of Dor and its villages, or the inhabitants of Yivle'am and its villages, or the inhabitants of Megiddo and its villages, for the Kena'anites persisted in dwelling in that land.

28 When Yisra'EL grew strong, they put the Kena'anites to forced labor, but did not drive them out completely.

29 And Efrayim did not drive out the Kena'anites who lived in Gezer, so the Kena'anites lived in Gezer among them.

30 Zevulun did not drive out the inhabitants of Kitron, or the inhabitants of Nahalol, so the Kena'anites lived among them, but became subject to forced labor.

31 Asher did not drive out the inhabitants of Akko, or the inhabitants of Tzidon or of Akhlav or of Akhziv or of Khelbah or of Afik or of Rekhov,

32 So the Asherites lived among the Kena'anites, the inhabitants of the land, for they did not drive them out.

33 Naftali did not drive out the inhabitants of Beit-Shemesh, or the inhabitants of Beit-Anat, so they lived among the Kena'anites, the inhabitants of the land. Nevertheless, the inhabitants of Beit-Shemesh and of Beit-Anat became subject to forced labor for them.

34 The Amorites pressed the people of Dan back into the hill country, for they did not allow them to come down to the plain.

35 The Amorites persisted in dwelling in Mount Kheres, in Ayalon, and in Sha'albim, but the hand of the house of Yosef rested heavily on them, and they became subject to forced labor.

36 And the border of the Amorites ran from the ascent of Akrabbim, from Sela and upward.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) and 4QJudgb (4Q50) from Cave 4 are the primary DSS witnesses for Shofetim. Chapter 1 opens the book by documenting the systematic failure to complete the conquest commanded in Yehoshua — tribe after tribe failing to drive out the Kena'anites. The DSS text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Shofetim in full as part of the canonical books. Chapter 1 is read as the covenant diagnosis of the entire period of the Judges: the partial obedience that replaced complete covenant faithfulness becomes the root of every subsequent crisis.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 1. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The repeated formula — did not drive out — appears seven times in verses 21-33, cataloging tribe by tribe the failure to complete the Mosaic commission.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — YHWH was with Yehudah, and he took possession of the hill country, but he could not drive out the inhabitants of the plain because they had chariots of iron. YHWH was present and Yehudah still failed. The iron chariots are the excuse — but Yehoshua had already addressed this objection directly in Yehoshua 17:18: drive them out though they have iron chariots and though they are strong. The failure was not military impossibility — it was covenant unwillingness. The chariots of iron did not stop Yehudah. The will to complete the covenant command did. Every subsequent crisis in Shofetim is planted in the soil of Chapter 1.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 2 The Angel of YHWH at Bokhim — A Generation That Did Not Know YHWH — The Pattern of the Judges

1 Now the angel of YHWH went up from Gilgal to Bokhim. And he said, I brought you up from Egypt and brought you into the land that I swore to give to your fathers. I said, I will never break my covenant with you,

2 And you shall make no covenant with the inhabitants of this land — you shall break down their altars. But you have not obeyed my voice. What is this you have done?

3 So now I say, I will not drive them out before you, but they shall become thorns in your sides, and their gods shall be a snare to you.

4 As soon as the angel of YHWH spoke these words to all the people of Yisra'EL, the people lifted up their voices and wept.

5 And they called the name of that place Bokhim. And they sacrificed there to YHWH.

6 When Yehoshua dismissed the people, the people of Yisra'EL went each to his inheritance to take possession of the land.

7 And the people served YHWH all the days of Yehoshua, and all the days of the elders who outlived Yehoshua, who had seen all the great work that YHWH had done for Yisra'EL.

8 And Yehoshua the son of Nun, the servant of YHWH, died at the age of 110 years.

9 And they buried him within the boundaries of his inheritance in Timnat-Kheres, in the hill country of Efrayim, north of the mountain of Ga'ash.

10 And all that generation also were gathered to their fathers. And there arose another generation after them who did not know YHWH or the work that he had done for Yisra'EL.

11 And the people of Yisra'EL did what was evil in the sight of YHWH and served the Ba'als.

12 And they abandoned YHWH, the Creator of their fathers, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt. They went after other gods, from among the gods of the peoples who were around them, and bowed down to them. And they provoked YHWH to anger.

13 They abandoned YHWH and served the Ba'als and the Ashtarots.

14 So the anger of YHWH was kindled against Yisra'EL, and he gave them over to plunderers, who plundered them. And he sold them into the hand of their surrounding enemies, so that they could no longer withstand their enemies.

15 Whenever they marched out, the hand of YHWH was against them for harm, as YHWH had warned, and as YHWH had sworn to them. And they were in terrible distress.

16 Then YHWH raised up judges, who saved them out of the hand of those who plundered them.

17 Yet they did not listen to their judges, for they whored after other gods and bowed down to them. They soon turned aside from the way in which their fathers had walked, who had obeyed the commandments of YHWH, and they did not do so.

18 Whenever YHWH raised up judges for them, YHWH was with the judge, and he saved them from the hand of their enemies all the days of the judge. For YHWH was moved to pity by their groaning because of those who afflicted and oppressed them.

19 But whenever the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers, going after other gods, serving them and bowing down to them. They did not drop any of their practices or their stubborn ways.

20 So the anger of YHWH was kindled against Yisra'EL, and he said, Because this people have transgressed my covenant that I commanded their fathers and have not obeyed my voice,

21 I will no longer drive out before them any of the nations that Yehoshua left when he died,

22 In order to test Yisra'EL by them, whether they will take care to walk in the way of YHWH as their fathers did, or not.

23 So YHWH left those nations, not driving them out quickly, and he did not give them into the hand of Yehoshua.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Shofetim. Chapter 2 provides the theological framework for the entire book: the cycle of apostasy, oppression, cry, deliverance, and return to apostasy. Verse 10 — a generation that did not know YHWH — is the covenant diagnosis of every generation that fails to transmit the covenant memory to its children.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 2 in full. Verse 16 — then YHWH raised up judges who saved them — is read as the declaration of YHWH's persistent covenant mercy: despite repeated betrayal, YHWH continues to raise up deliverers. The mercy precedes and exceeds the faithfulness.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 2. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The cycle established in Chapter 2 — apostasy, oppression, cry, judge, deliverance, rest, death of judge, renewed apostasy — governs every subsequent narrative in the book.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — there arose another generation who did not know YHWH or the work that he had done for Yisra'EL. The covenant catastrophe of Shofetim begins with a single generation that did not know. The transmission failed. The stones that should have generated questions — Yehoshua 4:6 — did not. The stories that should have been told were not told. The covenant memory that Moshe commanded in Devarim 6:20-25 was not passed on. Verse 19 — whenever the judge died, they turned back and were more corrupt than their fathers. Each cycle is not a return to the same point — it is a descent. The corruption deepens with each generation. Shofetim is not a static cycle — it is a downward spiral.

THE FIRST JUDGES — OTNI'EL — EHUD — SHAMGAR

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 3 Otni'el — Ehud — Shamgar — The First Three Judges

1 Now these are the nations that YHWH left, to test Yisra'EL by them, that is, all in Yisra'EL who had not experienced all the wars in Kena'an.

2 It was only in order that the generations of the people of Yisra'EL might know war, to teach war to those who had not known it before.

3 These are the nations: the five lords of the Pelishtim, and all the Kena'anites and the Tzidonians and the Khivvites who lived on Mount Lebanon, from Mount Ba'al-Hermon as far as Lebo-Khamath.

4 They were for the testing of Yisra'EL, to know whether Yisra'EL would obey the commandments of YHWH, which he commanded their fathers by the hand of Moshe.

5 So the people of Yisra'EL lived among the Kena'anites, the Khittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Khivvites, and the Yevusites.

6 And their daughters they took to themselves for wives, and their own daughters they gave to their sons, and they served their gods.

7 And the people of Yisra'EL did what was evil in the sight of YHWH. They forgot YHWH their Creator and served the Ba'als and the Asherot.

8 Therefore the anger of YHWH was kindled against Yisra'EL, and he sold them into the hand of Kushan-Rishatayim king of Mesopotamia. And the people of Yisra'EL served Kushan-Rishatayim eight years.

9 But when the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH, YHWH raised up a deliverer for the people of Yisra'EL, who saved them — Otni'el the son of Kenaz, Kalev's younger brother.

10 The Spirit of YHWH was upon him, and he judged Yisra'EL. He went out to war, and YHWH gave Kushan-Rishatayim king of Mesopotamia into his hand. And his hand prevailed over Kushan-Rishatayim.

11 So the land had rest forty years. Then Otni'el the son of Kenaz died.

12 And the people of Yisra'EL again did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, and YHWH strengthened Eglon the king of Moav against Yisra'EL, because they had done what was evil in the sight of YHWH.

13 He gathered to himself the Ammonites and the Amalekites, and went and defeated Yisra'EL. And they took possession of the city of palms.

14 And the people of Yisra'EL served Eglon the king of Moav eighteen years.

15 But when the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH, YHWH raised up for them a deliverer — Ehud, the son of Gera, the Binyaminite, a left-handed man. The people of Yisra'EL sent tribute by him to Eglon the king of Moav.

16 And Ehud made for himself a sword with two edges, a cubit in length, and he bound it on his right thigh under his clothes.

17 And he presented the tribute to Eglon king of Moav. Now Eglon was a very fat man.

18 And when Ehud had finished presenting the tribute, he sent away the people who carried the tribute.

19 But he himself turned back at the idols near Gilgal and said, I have a secret message for you, O king. And he commanded, Silence. And all his attendants went out from his presence.

20 And Ehud came to him as he was sitting alone in his cool roof chamber. And Ehud said, I have a message from Creator for you. And he arose from his seat.

21 And Ehud reached with his left hand, took the sword from his right thigh, and thrust it into his belly.

22 And the hilt also went in after the blade, and the fat closed over the blade, for he did not pull the sword out of his belly, and the dung came out.

23 Then Ehud went out into the porch and closed the doors of the roof chamber behind him and locked them.

24 When he had gone, the servants came, and when they saw that the doors of the roof chamber were locked, they thought, Surely he is relieving himself in the closet of the cool chamber.

25 And they waited till they were embarrassed. But when he still did not open the doors of the roof chamber, they took the key and opened them, and there was their master fallen dead on the floor.

26 Ehud escaped while they delayed, and he passed beyond the idols and escaped to Se'irah.

27 When he arrived, he sounded the trumpet in the hill country of Efrayim. Then the people of Yisra'EL went down with him from the hill country, and he was their leader.

28 And he said to them, Follow after me, for YHWH has given your enemies the Moavites into your hand. So they went down after him and seized the fords of the Yarden against the Moavites and did not allow anyone to pass over.

29 And they killed at that time about ten thousand of the Moavites, all strong, able-bodied men. Not a man escaped.

30 So Moav was subdued that day under the hand of Yisra'EL. And the land had rest eighty years.

31 After him was Shamgar the son of Anat, who killed six hundred of the Pelishtim with an oxgoad, and he also saved Yisra'EL.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 3. The first judge cycle is established through Otni'el — the model judge — followed by the vivid narrative of Ehud's assassination of Eglon. The Spirit of YHWH empowering the judge — verse 10 — is the covenant mechanism of every deliverance in Shofetim.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 3 in full. The account of Shamgar — verse 31 — a single verse describing the killing of six hundred Pelishtim with an oxgoad, is read as the covenant principle that YHWH can use any instrument — even an agricultural tool — to deliver his people.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 3. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Otni'el is the ideal judge — he appears, the Spirit comes upon him, he delivers, the land rests. No moral failures, no complications. He is the covenant standard against which every subsequent judge falls short.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 10 — the Spirit of YHWH was upon him, and he judged Yisra'EL. The Spirit of YHWH empowering the deliverer is the theological engine of every judge cycle. The judge does not deliver by military genius or political power alone — the Spirit of YHWH is the source of the deliverance. Ehud's account — verses 15-30 — is one of the most vivid and darkly humorous narratives in the Nevi'im. The left-handed Binyaminite, the concealed double-edged sword, the fat king, the locked door, the servants waiting in embarrassment. The narrative's earthiness does not diminish its theology: YHWH works through Ehud's cunning to deliver Yisra'EL from an oppressor they brought upon themselves through covenant failure.

DEVORAH AND BARAK — CHAPTERS 4 THROUGH 5

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 4 Devorah and Barak — Ya'el and Sisera — YHWH Routed Sisera Before Yisra'EL

1 And the people of Yisra'EL again did what was evil in the sight of YHWH after Ehud died.

2 And YHWH sold them into the hand of Yavin king of Kena'an, who reigned in Khatzor. The commander of his army was Sisera, who lived in Kharoshet-Haggoyim.

3 Then the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH for help, for he had nine hundred chariots of iron and he oppressed the people of Yisra'EL cruelly for twenty years.

4 Now Devorah, a prophetess, the wife of Lappidot, was judging Yisra'EL at that time.

5 She used to sit under the palm of Devorah between Ramah and Beit-El in the hill country of Efrayim, and the people of Yisra'EL came up to her for judgment.

6 She sent and summoned Barak the son of Avino'am from Kedesh-Naftali and said to him, Has not YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, commanded you, Go, gather your men at Mount Tavor, taking ten thousand from the people of Naftali and the people of Zevulun.

7 And I will draw out Sisera, the general of Yavin's army, to meet you by the river Kishon with his chariots and his troops, and I will give him into your hand?

8 Barak said to her, If you will go with me, I will go, but if you will not go with me, I will not go.

9 And she said, I will surely go with you. Nevertheless, the road on which you are going will not lead to your glory, for YHWH will sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. Then Devorah arose and went with Barak to Kedesh.

10 And Barak called out Zevulun and Naftali to Kedesh. And ten thousand men went up at his heels, and Devorah went up with him.

11 Now Khever the Kenite had separated from the Kenites, the descendants of Khovav the father-in-law of Moshe, and had pitched his tent as far away as the oak in Tza'ananim, which is near Kedesh.

12 When Sisera was told that Barak the son of Avino'am had gone up to Mount Tavor,

13 Sisera called out all his chariots, nine hundred chariots of iron, and all the men who were with him, from Kharoshet-Haggoyim to the river Kishon.

14 And Devorah said to Barak, Up, for this is the day in which YHWH has given Sisera into your hand. Does not YHWH go out before you? So Barak went down from Mount Tavor with ten thousand men following him.

15 And YHWH routed Sisera and all his chariots and all his army before Barak by the edge of the sword. And Sisera got down from his chariot and fled away on foot.

16 And Barak pursued the chariots and the army to Kharoshet-Haggoyim, and all the army of Sisera fell by the edge of the sword — not a man was left.

17 But Sisera fled away on foot to the tent of Ya'el, the wife of Khever the Kenite, for there was peace between Yavin the king of Khatzor and the house of Khever the Kenite.

18 And Ya'el came out to meet Sisera and said to him, Turn aside, my lord, turn aside to me — do not be afraid. So he turned aside to her into the tent, and she covered him with a rug.

19 And he said to her, Please give me a little water to drink, for I am thirsty. So she opened a skin of milk and gave him a drink and covered him.

20 And he said to her, Stand at the opening of the tent, and if any man comes and asks you, Is anyone here? say, No.

21 But Ya'el the wife of Khever took a tent peg, and took a hammer in her hand. Then she went softly to him and drove the peg into his temple until it went down into the ground while he was lying fast asleep from weariness. So he died.

22 And behold, as Barak was pursuing Sisera, Ya'el went out to meet him and said to him, Come, and I will show you the man whom you are seeking. So he went in to her tent, and there lay Sisera dead, with the tent peg in his temple.

23 So on that day Creator subdued Yavin the king of Kena'an before the people of Yisra'EL.

24 And the hand of the people of Yisra'EL pressed harder and harder against Yavin the king of Kena'an, until they destroyed Yavin king of Kena'an.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 4. Devorah is the only female judge in Shofetim and one of the few women explicitly designated as a prophetess in the Nevi'im. The prose account of Chapter 4 is followed by Devorah's victory song in Chapter 5 — one of the oldest poems in the Hebrew canon.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 4 in full. Ya'el's killing of Sisera with a tent peg — verse 21 — is read as the fulfillment of Devorah's prophecy in verse 9: YHWH will sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. The covenant promise was specific and it was kept through the most unexpected instrument.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 4. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 15 — YHWH routed Sisera and all his chariots — the nine hundred iron chariots that Barak feared — before Barak. The military technology that represented insurmountable odds became irrelevant when YHWH fought.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8-9 — Barak said, If you will go with me, I will go, but if you will not go with me, I will not go. Devorah's response is the covenant consequence of conditional obedience: nevertheless, the road on which you are going will not lead to your glory — for YHWH will sell Sisera into the hand of a woman. Barak's faith required the physical presence of the prophetess — he would not go on YHWH's word alone. The victory is still given — YHWH is faithful despite Barak's conditional faith — but the glory goes to Ya'el. The covenant principle: YHWH accomplishes his purpose through whatever instrument is willing when his appointed instrument hesitates.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 5 The Song of Devorah — Praised Above Women Shall Ya'el Be — One of the Oldest Poems in the Hebrew Canon

1 Then sang Devorah and Barak the son of Avino'am on that day:

2 That the leaders took the lead in Yisra'EL, that the people offered themselves willingly, bless YHWH.

3 Hear, O kings — give ear, O princes — to YHWH I will sing — I will make melody to YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

4 YHWH, when you went out from Se'ir, when you marched from the region of Edom, the earth trembled and the heavens dropped, yes, the clouds dropped water.

5 The mountains quaked before YHWH, even Sinai before YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL.

6 In the days of Shamgar, son of Anat, in the days of Ya'el, the highways were abandoned, and travelers kept to the byways.

7 The villagers ceased in Yisra'EL — they ceased to be — until I arose, Devorah arose, a mother in Yisra'EL.

8 When new gods were chosen, then war was in the gates. Was shield or spear to be seen among forty thousand in Yisra'EL?

9 My heart goes out to the commanders of Yisra'EL who offered themselves willingly among the people. Bless YHWH.

10 Tell of it, you who ride on white donkeys, you who sit on rich carpets and you who walk by the way.

11 To the sound of musicians at the watering places, there they repeat the righteous triumphs of YHWH, the righteous triumphs of his villagers in Yisra'EL. Then down to the gates marched the people of YHWH.

12 Awake, awake, Devorah — awake, awake, break out in a song. Arise, Barak, lead away your captives, O son of Avino'am.

13 Then down marched the remnant of the noble — the people of YHWH marched down for me against the mighty.

14 From Efrayim their root they marched down into the valley, following you, Binyamin, with your kinsmen — from Makhir marched down the commanders, and from Zevulun those who bear the lieutenant's staff.

15 The princes of Yissaskhar came with Devorah, and Yissaskhar faithful to Barak — into the valley they rushed at his heels. Among the clans of Reuven there were great searchings of heart.

16 Why did you sit still among the sheepfolds, to hear the whistling for the flocks? Among the clans of Reuven there were great searchings of heart.

17 Gil'ad stayed beyond the Yarden — and Dan, why did he stay with the ships? Asher sat still at the coast of the sea, staying by his landings.

18 Zevulun is a people who risked their lives to the death — Naftali too, on the heights of the field.

19 The kings came, they fought — then fought the kings of Kena'an, at Ta'anakh, by the waters of Megiddo — they got no spoils of silver.

20 From heaven the stars fought, from their courses they fought against Sisera.

21 The torrent Kishon swept them away, the ancient torrent, the torrent Kishon. March on, my soul, with might.

22 Then loud beat the horses' hoofs with the galloping, galloping of his steeds.

23 Curse Meroz, says the angel of YHWH, curse its inhabitants thoroughly, because they did not come to the help of YHWH, to the help of YHWH against the mighty.

24 Most blessed of women be Ya'el, the wife of Khever the Kenite, of tent-dwelling women most blessed.

25 He asked for water and she gave him milk — she brought him curds in a noble's bowl.

26 She sent her hand to the tent peg and her right hand to the workmen's mallet — she struck Sisera, she crushed his head, she shattered and pierced his temple.

27 Between her feet he sank, he fell, he lay still — between her feet he sank, he fell — where he sank, there he fell dead.

28 Out of the window she peered, the mother of Sisera wailed through the lattice: Why is his chariot so long in coming? Why tarry the hoofbeats of his chariots?

29 Her wisest princesses answer — indeed, she answers herself:

30 Have they not found and divided the spoil? — a womb or two for every man — spoil of dyed materials for Sisera, spoil of dyed materials embroidered, two pieces of dyed work embroidered for the neck as spoil?

31 So may all your enemies perish, O YHWH. But your friends be like the sun as he rises in his might. And the land had rest forty years.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 5. The Song of Devorah is widely recognized as one of the oldest compositions in the Hebrew canon, possibly from the 12th century BCE. Its archaic Hebrew vocabulary and poetic structure place it among the earliest strata of biblical literature alongside the Song of the Sea in Shemot 15.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 5 in full. The Song of Devorah is read as the covenant victory hymn — the prose account of Chapter 4 sung in poetic triumph. Verse 24 — most blessed of women be Ya'el — is the covenant honor declaration for the woman who acted when the covenant's appointed warrior hesitated.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 5. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 20 — from heaven the stars fought, from their courses they fought against Sisera — reflects the same covenant warfare theology as the War Scroll: the heavenly host participates alongside the human covenant forces.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 23 — curse Meroz, says the angel of YHWH, curse its inhabitants thoroughly, because they did not come to the help of YHWH, to the help of YHWH against the mighty. Meroz is cursed not for fighting against YHWH but for failing to act when action was required. Passive neutrality when covenant warfare is underway is treated as covenant betrayal. Verses 28-30 — the mother of Sisera waiting at the window — is one of the most devastating pieces of irony in the Hebrew canon. While Ya'el's act is celebrated in verse 24, Sisera's mother imagines her son distributing the spoil — a womb or two for every man. The contrast between the reality and the expectation, between Ya'el's triumph and the mother's delusion, is the poem's moral weight.

GID'ON — CHAPTERS 6 THROUGH 7

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 6 Gideon Called by the Angel of YHWH — The Fleece — Is YHWH With Us?

1 The people of Yisra'EL did what was evil in the sight of YHWH, and YHWH gave them into the hand of Midyan seven years.

2 And the hand of Midyan overpowered Yisra'EL, and because of Midyan the people of Yisra'EL made for themselves the dens that are in the mountains and the caves and the strongholds.

3 For whenever the people of Yisra'EL planted crops, the Midyanites and the Amalekites and the people of the East would come up against them.

4 They would encamp against them and devour the produce of the land, as far as Azzah, and leave no sustenance in Yisra'EL and no sheep or ox or donkey.

5 For they would come up with their livestock and their tents — they would come like locusts in number, both they and their camels could not be counted. So they laid waste the land as they came in.

6 And Yisra'EL was brought very low because of Midyan. And the people of Yisra'EL cried out for help to YHWH.

7 When the people of Yisra'EL cried out to YHWH on account of the Midyanites,

8 YHWH sent a prophet to the people of Yisra'EL. And he said to them, Thus says YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL: I led you up from Egypt and brought you out of the house of slavery.

9 And I delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of all who oppressed you, and drove them out before you and gave you their land.

10 And I said to you, I am YHWH your Creator — you shall not fear the gods of the Amorites in whose land you dwell. But you have not obeyed my voice.

11 Now the angel of YHWH came and sat under the terebinth at Ofrah, which belonged to Yo'ash the Aviezrite, while his son Gid'on was beating out wheat in the winepress to hide it from the Midyanites.

12 And the angel of YHWH appeared to him and said to him, YHWH is with you, O mighty man of valor.

13 And Gid'on said to him, Please, my lord, if YHWH is with us, why then has all this happened to us? And where are all his wonderful deeds that our fathers recounted to us, saying, Did not YHWH bring us up from Egypt? But now YHWH has forsaken us and given us into the hand of Midyan.

14 And YHWH turned to him and said, Go in this might of yours and save Yisra'EL from the hand of Midyan — do I not send you?

15 And he said to him, Please, Lord, how can I save Yisra'EL? Behold, my clan is the weakest in Menasheh, and I am the least in my father's house.

16 And YHWH said to him, But I will be with you, and you shall strike the Midyanites as one man.

17 And he said to him, If now I have found favor in your eyes, then show me a sign that it is you who speak with me.

18 Please do not depart from here until I come to you and bring out my present and set it before you. And he said, I will stay till you return.

19 So Gid'on went into his house and prepared a young goat and unleavened cakes from an ephah of flour. The meat he put in a basket, and the broth he put in a pot, and brought them to him under the terebinth and presented them.

20 And the angel of Creator said to him, Take the meat and the unleavened cakes, and put them on this rock, and pour the broth over them. And he did so.

21 Then the angel of YHWH reached out the tip of the staff that was in his hand and touched the meat and the unleavened cakes. And fire sprang up from the rock and consumed the meat and the unleavened cakes. And the angel of YHWH vanished from his sight.

22 Then Gid'on perceived that he was the angel of YHWH. And Gid'on said, Alas, O YHWH Creator, for now I have seen the angel of YHWH face to face.

23 But YHWH said to him, Peace be to you. Do not fear — you shall not die.

24 Then Gid'on built an altar there to YHWH and called it, YHWH Is Peace. To this day it still stands at Ofrah, which belongs to the Aviezrites.

25 That night YHWH said to him, Take your father's bull, and the second bull seven years old, and pull down the altar of Ba'al that your father has, and cut down the Asherah that is beside it

26 And build an altar to YHWH your Creator on the top of the stronghold here, with stones laid in due order. Then take the second bull and offer it as a burnt offering with the wood of the Asherah that you shall cut down.

27 So Gid'on took ten men of his servants and did as YHWH had told him. But because he was too afraid of his father's household and the men of the town to do it by day, he did it by night.

28 When the men of the town rose early in the morning, behold, the altar of Ba'al was broken down, and the Asherah beside it was cut down, and the second bull was offered on the altar that had been built.

29 And they said to one another, Who has done this thing? And after they had searched and inquired, they said, Gid'on the son of Yo'ash has done this thing.

30 Then the men of the town said to Yo'ash, Bring out your son, that he may die, for he has broken down the altar of Ba'al and cut down the Asherah beside it.

31 But Yo'ash said to all who stood against him, Will you contend for Ba'al? Or will you save him? Whoever contends for him shall be put to death by morning. If he is a god, let him contend for himself, because his altar has been broken down.

32 Therefore on that day Gid'on was called Yeruba'al, that is to say, Let Ba'al contend against him, because he broke down his altar.

33 Now all the Midyanites and the Amalekites and the people of the East came together, and they crossed the Yarden and encamped in the Valley of Yizre'el.

34 But the Spirit of YHWH clothed Gid'on, and he sounded the trumpet, and the Aviezrites were called out to follow him.

35 And he sent messengers throughout all Menasheh, and they too were called out to follow him. And he sent messengers to Asher, Zevulun, and Naftali, and they went up to meet them.

36 Then Gid'on said to Creator, If you will save Yisra'EL by my hand, as you have said,

37 Behold, I am laying a fleece of wool on the threshing floor. If there is dew on the fleece alone, and it is dry on all the ground, then I shall know that you will save Yisra'EL by my hand, as you have said.

38 And it was so. When he rose early next morning and squeezed the fleece, he wrung enough dew from the fleece to fill a bowl with water.

39 Then Gid'on said to Creator, Let not your anger burn against me — let me speak just once more. Please let me test just once more with the fleece. Please let it be dry on the fleece only, and on all the ground let there be dew.

40 And Creator did so that night — and it was dry on the fleece only, and on all the ground there was dew.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 6. The calling of Gid'on is one of the most extended judge-calling narratives in Shofetim. The angel of YHWH greeting Gid'on — YHWH is with you, O mighty man of valor — while Gid'on hides wheat from Midyanites captures the irony of covenant calling: YHWH addresses people according to what he is making them, not what they currently are.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 6 in full. Gid'on's question in verse 13 — if YHWH is with us, why then has all this happened to us? — is read as the paradigmatic covenant lament: the gap between the covenant promises of the fathers and the present covenant reality. YHWH does not rebuke the question — he answers it with a commission.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 6. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. Verse 34 — the Spirit of YHWH clothed Gid'on — uses the Hebrew lavshah, to clothe. The Spirit does not merely come upon Gid'on — he wears him as a garment. The judge becomes the instrument the Spirit inhabits.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 13 — if YHWH is with us, why then has all this happened to us? Gid'on's question is honest covenant theology: if the covenant promises are real, why does the covenant reality look like this? YHWH's answer — verse 14 — is not a theological explanation but a commission: go in this might of yours and save Yisra'EL. The answer to the problem of covenant suffering is covenant action. Verse 15 — my clan is the weakest in Menasheh, and I am the least in my father's house. Every judge calling in Shofetim involves YHWH choosing the least likely instrument. The pattern is deliberate: the deliverance cannot be attributed to human strength or status. YHWH's response — verse 16 — but I will be with you.

SHOFETIM — CHAPTER 7 Three Hundred Men — Torches and Jars — YHWH Has Given the Midyanite Camp Into Your Hand

1 Then Yeruba'al — that is, Gid'on — and all the people who were with him rose early and encamped beside the spring of Kharod. And the camp of Midyan was north of them, by the hill of Moreh, in the valley.

2 YHWH said to Gid'on, The people with you are too many for me to give the Midyanites into their hand, lest Yisra'EL boast over me, saying, My own hand has saved me.

3 Now therefore proclaim in the ears of the people, saying, Whoever is fearful and trembling, let him return home and hurry away from Mount Gilead. Then twenty-two thousand of the people returned, and ten thousand remained.

4 And YHWH said to Gid'on, The people are still too many. Take them down to the water, and I will test them for you there, and anyone of whom I say to you, This one shall go with you, shall go with you, and anyone of whom I say to you, This one shall not go with you, shall not go.

5 So he brought the people down to the water. And YHWH said to Gid'on, Every one who laps the water with his tongue, as a dog laps, you shall set by himself. Likewise, every one who kneels down to drink.

6 And the number of those who lapped, putting their hands to their mouths, was three hundred men, but all the rest of the people knelt down to drink water.

7 And YHWH said to Gid'on, With the three hundred men who lapped I will save you and give the Midyanites into your hand, and let all the others go every man to his home.

8 So the people took provisions in their hands, and their trumpets. And he sent all the rest of Yisra'EL every man to his tent, but retained the three hundred men. And the camp of Midyan was below him in the valley.

9 That same night YHWH said to him, Arise, go down against the camp, for I have given it into your hand.

10 But if you are afraid to go down, go down to the camp with Purah your servant.

11 And you shall hear what they say, and afterward your hands shall be strengthened to go down against the camp. Then he went down with Purah his servant to the outposts of the armed men who were in the camp.

12 And the Midyanites and the Amalekites and all the people of the East lay along the valley like locusts in abundance, and their camels were without number, as the sand that is on the seashore in abundance.

13 When Gid'on came, behold, a man was telling a dream to his comrade. And he said, Behold, I dreamed a dream, and behold, a cake of barley bread tumbled into the camp of Midyan and came to the tent and struck it so that it fell and turned it upside down, so that the tent lay flat.

14 And his comrade answered, This is no other than the sword of Gid'on the son of Yo'ash, a man of Yisra'EL. Creator has given into his hand Midyan and all the camp.

15 As soon as Gid'on heard the telling of the dream and its interpretation, he worshiped. And he returned to the camp of Yisra'EL and said, Arise, for YHWH has given the host of Midyan into your hand.

16 And he divided the three hundred men into three companies and put trumpets into the hands of all of them and empty jars, with torches inside the jars.

17 And he said to them, Look at me, and do likewise. When I come to the outskirts of the camp, do as I do.

18 When I blow the trumpet, I and all who are with me, then blow the trumpets also on every side of all the camp and shout, For YHWH and for Gid'on.

19 So Gid'on and the hundred men who were with him came to the outskirts of the camp at the beginning of the middle watch, when they had just set the watch. And they blew the trumpets and smashed the jars that were in their hands.

20 Then the three companies blew the trumpets and broke the jars. They held in their left hands the torches, and in their right hands the trumpets to blow. And they cried out, A sword for YHWH and for Gid'on.

21 Every man stood in his place around the camp, and all the army ran — they cried out and fled.

22 When they blew the three hundred trumpets, YHWH set every man's sword against his comrade and against all the army. And the army fled as far as Beit-Hashitah toward Tzerera, as far as the border of Avel-Mekholah, by Tabbat.

23 And the men of Yisra'EL were called out from Naftali and from Asher and from all Menasheh, and they pursued after Midyan.

24 Gid'on sent messengers throughout all the hill country of Efrayim, saying, Come down against the Midyanites and capture the waters against them, as far as Beit-Barah, and also the Yarden. So all the men of Efrayim were called out, and they captured the waters as far as Beit-Barah, and also the Yarden.

25 And they captured the two princes of Midyan, Orev and Ze'ev. They killed Orev at the rock of Orev, and Ze'ev they killed at the winepress of Ze'ev. Then they pursued Midyan, and they brought the heads of Orev and Ze'ev to Gid'on across the Yarden.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 4QJudga (4Q49) preserves fragmentary portions of Chapter 7. The reduction from thirty-two thousand to three hundred — verses 2-7 — is the most concentrated covenant theology of YHWH's warfare in Shofetim: the army is reduced precisely so that Yisra'EL cannot claim self-deliverance. The victory must be attributed to YHWH alone.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Chapter 7 in full. The barley bread dream in verses 13-14 — a loaf of barley tumbling into the Midyanite camp and overturning a tent — is read as the covenant sign that YHWH interprets his own purposes through the dreams of the enemy. Even the enemy's subconscious testifies to YHWH's sovereignty.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Shofetim Chapter 7. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading. The three companies of one hundred, the trumpets, the empty jars, and the torches — verses 16-20 — constitute a covenant battle liturgy: sound, light, shout — no sword in the initial assault. YHWH does the fighting.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — the people with you are too many for me to give the Midyanites into their hand, lest Yisra'EL boast over me, saying, my own hand has saved me. YHWH explicitly reduces his own army to prevent the covenant people from misattributing the victory. The theological principle is stated plainly: if the numbers were sufficient for natural explanation, the covenant community would credit itself. YHWH strips away every natural explanation to make the source of the deliverance unmistakable. Three hundred men with empty jars and torches against the Midyanites like locusts in number — verse 12 — cannot be explained by military superiority. The victory belongs to YHWH by design.

END OF PART ONE — CHAPTERS 1 THROUGH 7
